coredata.dc:title: Statistical optimization of process parameters for improvement of phycobiliproteins (PBPs) yield using ultrasound-assisted extraction and its kinetic study
coredata.dc:creator: Sharma, Ramesh
Bhunia, Biswanath
Mondal, Abhijit
Kanti Bandyopadhyay, Tarun
Devi, Indrama
Oinam, Gunapati
Prasanna, Radha
Abraham, G.
Nath Tiwari, Onkar
coredata.dc:description: The present study is mainly concerned for the development of an optimal ultrasound-assisted extraction (UAE) condition for phycobiliproteins (PBPs) from Oscillatoria sp. (BTA 170) using Taguchi methodology. Four process parameters viz. solid to liquid ratio, duty cycle, electrical acoustic intensity, and pH, for UAE were optimized using Taguchi methodology for enhanced PBPs extraction. The ratio of signal to noise (S/N) was used to compute the optimized condition required to attain a higher yield of PBPs, the average performance of individual parameter and corresponding interactive effects. The statistically significant parameters with their contribution were assessed using Analysis of variance (ANOVA). Results showed that duty cycle contributed the maximum influence (30.81%) on phycocyanin (PC) extraction followed by a solid liquid ratio (28.62%), pH (22.46%) and electrical acoustic intensity (18.10%). The highest contribution on the extraction of phycoerythrin (PE) was found from pH (33.16%), followed by duty cycle (31.57%), solid to liquid ratio (22.83%) and electrical acoustic intensity (12.45%). For extraction of allophycocyanin (APC), the duty cycle, solid to liquid ratio, pH and electrical acoustic intensity contributed 29.47, 29.07, 29.03, and 12.43% respectively. Results obtained from Taguchi methodology indicated that enhanced PC (94.10%), PE (95.20%) and APC (90.54%) can be achieved with solid–liquid ratio (0.2 g/ml), electrical acoustic intensity (16.99 w/cm2), duty cycle (75%), and pH 7 than the yield of PBPs obtained under unoptimized condition. In the present study, higher yield of PC (38.99%), PE (20.84%), and APC (11.93%) were attained with UAE compared to yield obtained from homogenized Oscillatoria sp. BTA 170 using 0.05 M phosphate buffer. Batch extraction data of PBPs under UAE was fitted well with the second order model. The values of second-order rate constant (k) were computed as 6.66 × 10−4, 64.09 × 10−4 and 1.49 × 10−4 L/mg/min for extraction of PC, PE and APC respectively. The PBPs exhibited significant antioxidant property and hydrogen peroxide scavenging activity, which were increased with the enhancement of PBPs concentration.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phycobiliproteins
Optimization
Taguchi methodology
The ratio of signal-to-noise (S/N)
Analysis of variance (ANOVA)
Ultrasound-assisted extraction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of ultrasound-assisted extraction (UAE) by Taguchi methodology
Pbps extraction with selected parameters and levels (Phase II)
The analysis of data and performance prediction (Phase III)
Evaluation of antioxidant property of PBPs
Evaluation of scavenging activity of PBPs
Determination of antioxidant property and scavenging activity of PBPs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors would like to acknowledge to DBT, Govt. of India for financial support (BT/PR25035/ NER/95/ 973/ 2017).

This material is based upon research work supported by ICAR - IARI , New Delhi and NIT Agartala . Dr. O N Tiwari articulates thankfulness to HoD, Division of Microbiology of ICAR - IARI , New Delhi for inspiration and support. Dr. Oinam and Dr. Indrama would like to acknowledge to Director, DBT-IBSD , Imphal. Dr. Tarun kanti Bandyopadhyay, Dr. Abhijit Mondal and Dr. Biswanath Bhunia express gratitude to Director, National Institute of Technology Agartala for encouragement and support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the steps involved in the Taguchi DOE robust methodology.
Performance distribution of improved and current condition for (a) PC, (b) PE and (c) APC extraction from cyanobacteria using UAE.
The relationship of t/ct vs t from batch experimental extraction data of PC, PE and APC.

Antioxidant capacity of PC, PE and APC extracted from Oscillatoria sp. (BTA 170) compared with standard ascorbic acid and c) scavenging activities of PC, PE and APC extracted from Oscillatoria sp. (BTA 170) compared with standard gallic acid.
Experimental range of four variables studied using Taguchi methodology.
L9 OA (34) of design experiments for four factors for optimum PBPs extraction.
Main effects of selected parameters For PC, PE and APC.

head.ce:title: Statistical optimization of process parameters for improvement of phycobiliproteins (PBPs) yield using ultrasound-assisted extraction and its kinetic study

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre for Conservation and Utilisation of Blue Green Algae, Division of Microbiology, ICAR-Indian Agricultural Research Institute (ICAR-IARI), New Delhi 110012, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present study is mainly concerned for the development of an optimal ultrasound-assisted extraction (UAE) condition for phycobiliproteins (PBPs) from Oscillatoria sp. (BTA 170) using Taguchi methodology. Four process parameters viz. solid to liquid ratio, duty cycle, electrical acoustic intensity, and pH, for UAE were optimized using Taguchi methodology for enhanced PBPs extraction. The ratio of signal to noise (S/N) was used to compute the optimized condition required to attain a higher yield of PBPs, the average performance of individual parameter and corresponding interactive effects. The statistically significant parameters with their contribution were assessed using Analysis of variance (ANOVA). Results showed that duty cycle contributed the maximum influence (30.81%) on phycocyanin (PC) extraction followed by a solid liquid ratio (28.62%), pH (22.46%) and electrical acoustic intensity (18.10%). The highest contribution on the extraction of phycoerythrin (PE) was found from pH (33.16%), followed by duty cycle (31.57%), solid to liquid ratio (22.83%) and electrical acoustic intensity (12.45%). For extraction of allophycocyanin (APC), the duty cycle, solid to liquid ratio, pH and electrical acoustic intensity contributed 29.47, 29.07, 29.03, and 12.43% respectively. Results obtained from Taguchi methodology indicated that enhanced PC (94.10%), PE (95.20%) and APC (90.54%) can be achieved with solid–liquid ratio (0.2 g/ml), electrical acoustic intensity (16.99 w/cm2), duty cycle (75%), and pH 7 than the yield of PBPs obtained under unoptimized condition. In the present study, higher yield of PC (38.99%), PE (20.84%), and APC (11.93%) were attained with UAE compared to yield obtained from homogenized Oscillatoria sp. BTA 170 using 0.05 M phosphate buffer. Batch extraction data of PBPs under UAE was fitted well with the second order model. The values of second-order rate constant (k) were computed as 6.66 × 10−4, 64.09 × 10−4 and 1.49 × 10−4 L/mg/min for extraction of PC, PE and APC respectively. The PBPs exhibited significant antioxidant property and hydrogen peroxide scavenging activity, which were increased with the enhancement of PBPs concentration.

ce:section.ce:para: Cyanobacteria are a rich feedstock for naturally available antioxidant and are leading producer for the pigment in an environment [1–3]. The phycobiliproteins (PBPs) are colorant available in cyanobacteria and are considered as major supplementary proteins for photosynthesis. PBPs found inside the phycobilisomes (PBS) of cyanobacterial cell [4]. They displayed fluorescence properties due to the existence of a linear tetrapyrrole structure within PBPs molecules. Three types of PBPs available in cyanobacteria are phycoerythrin (PE), allophycocyanin (APC) and phycocyanin (PC). PC and PE show blue and reddish colour, respectively; however, APC displays bluish-green color [5]. They have a key role in transferring the electron during photosynthesis, as PBPs are major photosynthetic pigments [6,7]. In terms of energy capacity, PE is high energy pigments, followed by PC and APC having intermediate and low energy. PBPs are widely used in pharmaceuticals, food, and cosmetics industries as they display anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, hepatoprotective properties, and radical-scavenging [8,9]. PBPs are recently used in various diagnostic applications as they are non-toxic as well as fluorescent. Additionally, PBPs displayed higher coefficient in molar extinction with their greater quantum yield in fluorescence [10,11]. It is reported that 40% (w/w) of total protein of cyanobacteria is PBPs, which is highly soluble in water. Since PBPs are used extensively in various fields; focus on large scale production along with their inexpensive extraction method is warranted.

Extraction of PBPs from cyanobacteria signifies the most exciting research fields for various industries including pharmaceutical, food, and chemical industries. There are several extractions of PBPs from cyanobacteria do exists, such as maceration, soxhlet, percolation, and stirring used for separation of bioproducts. However, these conventional techniques used for extraction require more time and a higher number of manpower. Additionally, a large volume of organic solvents is required to achieve a significant amount of extraction. Presently, the development of ultrasound assisted extraction (UAE), microwave-and pressure-mediated extraction techniques are gaining momentum as they show higher extraction efficiency. Process parameters like solid to liquid ratio, duty cycle, electrical acoustic intensity, and pH play an important role in the extraction of PBPs under UAE [12]. Since the extraction yield of a bioactive compound depends on the amount of solvent used for extraction, therefore, solid to liquid ratio, play a significant role for attaining maximum yield [12]. The duty cycle and electrical acoustic intensity influence the enhanced exposure of acoustic cavitation, which helps to disrupt the cell and separation of intracellular compounds from cell. Therefore, the duty cycle and electrical acoustic intensity contribute a significant impact on the extraction of intracellular compounds from biomass [13,14]. Since most of the biological molecules are polar, therefore, the pH of the solution changes the three-dimensional (3D) arrangement through alteration of their charge. Hence, pH plays a critical role in their solubility, which modulates the extraction yield of bioactive compounds [15]. A huge number of experimental trials are performed commonly to optimize all the process factors and to evaluate the finest probable parametric conditions for accomplishing the higher yield. The conventional protocol used for optimization requires more time as one variable is optimized at a time (OVT) by keeping other factors ideal. Furthermore, OVT design are mainly focused on the average performance of the overall process [16]. Taguchi methodology is widely adopted to optimize various biochemical process variables. This methodology includes a specified set of self-governing parameters, which are uncontrollable and manageable within a precise area of importance [17,18]. Additionally, small scale experiment trial is effective over a complete experimental section [19]. Additionally, ANOVA can also be used to examine the experimental trial to evaluate the statistically noteworthy results at optimal levels of parameters. As per our information, limited works of research have been described on the optimization of process parameters for extraction of PBPs using Taguchi methodology. The most researches have used one variable per time methods to extract PBPs in UAE. Hence, the potential influence of the statistical optimization technique turn into our attention through implementation of Taguchi methodology in the area of PBPs extraction.

In our previous study, conventional extraction technique was applied to extract phycobiliproteins (PBPs) from Oscillatoria sp. BTA170 [20]. The aim of this present investigation is the improvement of the extraction yield of phycobiliproteins (PBPs) from Oscillatoria sp. BTA170 using ultrasound-assisted extraction (UAE). Here, the process parameters responsible for higher yield of PBPs were optimized using Taguchi methodology. The experiment trials were planned for four parameters at three points of OA design of L9 (34). Additionally, the kinetic evaluation of PBPs extraction in the batch process was assessed critically. The biochemical characterization of PBPs about the antioxidant property and hydrogen peroxide scavenging activity was also evaluated.

Oscillatoria sp. BTA170 was isolated from soil samples collected from paddy fields of Manipur having the latitude of N 24°52′09.0″ and longitude of E 94°1′12.9″ and characterized in our previously published manuscript [20] . The media used for maintenance of the above cyanobacteria was BG 11 solid media. BTA 170 was subculture after 7 days in BG 11 media to achieve cyanobacterium healthy strain.

The seed culture of cyanobacteria was performed in two hundred and fifty (2 5 0) ml of the conical flask and fifty (50) ml of BG 11 medium was taken. One full loop of BTA 170 culture was transferred aseptically and was incubated for five days at 28 °C. The light intensity and photoperiod were maintained were 4 k Lux and 12 h:12 h of day to night ratio, respectively. Manual agitation of the flask was carried out at 24 h of interval. 10% of the seed culture of Oscillatoria sp. BTA170 was transferred to 200 ml of BG 11 media [21] supplemented with 5 mM of sodium glutamate, and 0.3 M of glycine in 1000 ml of Erlenmeyer flask which was kept under 14 h:10 h (L:D) of photoperiod, and 6 k Lux of light intensity for 15 days. The samples withdrawn after 15 days of cultivation, were subjected to analyze the productivity of biomass and PBPs [20,22].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of ultrasound-assisted extraction (UAE) by Taguchi methodology
ce:section.ce:para: The Taguchi methodology includes setting up the various circumstances for the experiment performed through designing of orthogonal arrays (OA), which decrease the errors of experiment and improve the experimental reproducibility and efficiency. Since it minimizes the noise during the optimization process, Taguchi methodology has been considered as a robust experimental design [23] There are four distinct phases such as planning, conducting, analysis, and validation. Fig. 1 represents the process flow work of the Taguchi methodology. Every phase is given in Fig. 1, is distinctly separated by different objectives and connected with sequences to attain the complete optimization process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pbps extraction with selected parameters and levels (Phase II)
ce:section.ce:para: Determination of phycobiliproteins (PBPs) was carried out as per Tiwari et al., (2019) methodology [20]. All experiments were done in triplicate, and the mean value of experiments were reported.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The analysis of data and performance prediction (Phase III)
ce:section.ce:para:
Qualitek-4 software package developed by Nutek Inc., MI, USA, was used to analyze the experimental data. The effect of individual factors, multiple interactions within the designated parameters, optimum conditions determination, and the performance of overall extraction of PBEs was evaluated. The S/N was chosen bigger-is-better, which was considered as performance features for entire experiments. The ‘signal’ term of Taguchi methodology indicates the anticipated value (mean) and the ‘noise’ term denotes the disagreeable value (SD) for the characteristic of output [25]. Hence, the signal-to-noise (S/N) ratio is the ratio of the mean value of an experimental trial to the SD. The quality characteristics are measured by S/N ratio and indicated by deviation from the desired value. The deviation has been represented by loss function [L(y)] [26], which is calculated by L[y] = [k]x[y − m]2. In loss function, k represents the proportionality constant; m signifies the target value, and y represents the value obtained from experiments. For quality characteristics for bigger and better, the loss function represents as L[y] = [k] × [1/y2], and the estimated loss function can be denoted by
(1)
E
[ L
( y )
]
= k E
1 /
y 2
where Y is the value of output. E(1/y2) is estimated loss function, which was calculated from n number of sample and written as
(2)
∑
i = 1
n
1 /
y i 2
/ n

S/N ratio used in Taguchi methodology is a performance characteristics of a dynamic process to assess the robustness of the entire process [27]. The mathematical representation for the S/N ratio of ‘bigger is better,’ measure the deviance from the target and represented as mean square deviation (MSD) which is written by
(3)
Z = - 10 log
( M S D )
= - 10 log
∑
i = 1
n
1 /
y i 2
/ n / n

The methodology used for this study was validated with the data obtained from extraction experiments. The experiments were performed in predicted optimized extraction conditions obtained from the methodology.

Batch extraction of PC, PE, and APC was carried out at optimized extraction condition. UAE was carried out at room temperature for 20 min using 1 g of lyophilized powder of cyanobacterial biomass. The sample was collected after a specific time interval and amount of PC, PE, and APC were analyzed. The dissociation rate of PC, PE, and APC from cyanobacterial biomass were expressed by Eq. (4).
(4)
r =
dC
dt
= k
(
C S
-
C t
)
n
where, Ct represents the concentration of PC, PE and APC (mg/L) at time t (min), Cs indicates the saturated concentration of PC, PE and APC (mg/L) and t is denoted the extraction time (min). r, k, and n are the rate of extraction, the rate constant for extraction and order of reaction, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of antioxidant property of PBPs
ce:section.ce:para: The evaluation of antioxidant property of PBPs was carried out separately using Mitsuda, (1966) with slight modifications [28]. Sodium sulfate (28 mM) and ammonium molybdate (4 mM) were initially added in a prerequisite quantity of water. 0.6 M of sulfuric acid was added to the mixture, and the volume was adjusted to 250 ml. The various amounts of PBPs in the range of 100 µg to 1000 µg, were added with 2 ml of the mixture and kept for 15 min. The measurement of absorbance of the mixture was performed at 695 nm where ascorbic acid was used as standard [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of scavenging activity of PBPs
ce:section.ce:para:
The scavenging activity of PC, PE, and APC was carried out by Ruch et al. (1989) with slight modifications [29] where gallic acid was used as the standard. The preparation of 10 mM solution of hydrogen peroxide was performed in 0.1 M of phosphate buffer saline (pH 7.4), and different amount of PBPs in the range of 100–500 µg were added rapidly within 2 ml of said solution for 10 min. After mixing, the measurement of absorbance of the mixture was performed at a wavelength of 230 nm. The percentage of scavenging activity was computed by Eq. (5).
(5)
Scavenging (
% ) =
(
A C
-
A S
)
A C
X 100
The absorbance of control is designated with Ac. As indicates the absorbance of the sample or standard.
The nonlinear regression analysis of the data obtained from the experiment, was carried out by Graphpad Prism 5 software. A p-value less than or equal 0.05, was considered as significant value.

The influence of individual factor at the consigned level decided for measurement of PBPs extraction conditions is enlisted in Table 3 . Results show that the content of PBPs is reliant mostly on designated process parameters. Independently, solid to liquid ratio, duty cycle, electrical acoustic intensity, and pH are very important at Level 2 among the designated all process factors. Nevertheless, the comparative influence of the parameters is measured from the variation of magnitude in their average influence (L2-L1). The greater the difference of magnitude between their average influence indicates the strongest effect in yield [24]. Table 3 shows that pH exhibits the strongest influence on PC extraction, among other parameters. The electrical acoustic intensity shows the second strongest effect on the yield of PC, followed by solid to liquid ratio and duty cycle. Additionally, extraction of PE and APC also strongly depends on pH when compared with other factors, followed by electrical acoustic intensity, duty cycle, and solid to liquid ratio. It is also shown from the above sets of experiment trials that extraction of PC, PE, and APC is strongly reliant on the designated process factors. From Table 3, it is evident that a higher amount of PC, PE, and APC is found with a successive enhancement of all selected parameters up to Level 2. However, further enhancement of all designed factors (Level 3), the extraction amount of PC, PE and APC is then diminished. The extraction of PBPs is strongly depended on the amount of solid to solvent ration. Since polarity, viscosity, the vapour pressure of solvent play a significant role in extraction, therefore, selection of solvent is the important agenda before extraction. The formation of cavitation during extraction depends on the vapour pressure of the solvent. The cavitation is easily noticed in the presence of a low viscosity solvent, which indicates a higher rate of diffusion inside the porous solid. The polarity of the solvent is the important parameters as polar solvent influences the extraction of polar compounds [12]. In the present case, 0.05 M of Britton–Robinson buffer was used for extraction as PBPs is polar. It is obvious that the amount of PBPs depends on the volume of solvent; therefore, it also depends on solid to solvent ratio. It is reported that the increased duty cycle influences the enhanced exposure of acoustic cavitation, which increases the disruption of the cell surface. Since PBPs are intracellular protein, therefore, the enhanced duty cycle increases the extraction of PBPs. However, at 100% duty cycle (Level 3), the amount of PBPs extraction was found to decrease, which may be due to denaturation of the 3D structure of PBPs by acoustic cavitation [13]. Electrical acoustic intensity induces acoustic cavitation which turn induces disruption of the cell by increasing the mechanical effect. Therefore, the increase of electrical acoustic intensity also induces the separation of PBPs from the cell [14]. However, a further increase in electrical acoustic intensity (Level-3), the amount of PBPs is decreased as denaturation of PBPs takes place. It has been found that the pH of the solution changes the three-dimensional (3D) arrangement of protein as the charge of protein depends on the pH of the solvent [15]. Therefore, changing of pH from Level 1 to Level 2, separation of PBPs also increases. However, a further increase in pH (Level 3) decreases the yield of PBPs. Since the functional groups which are present in the surface of protein becomes have their distinct value of dissociation constant, therefore, the pH of the solution near the isoelectric point (pI) may decrease the separation.

The computation of severity index (SI) was performed using the Taguchi methodology, which signifies the effect of interaction between two distinct factors at different designated levels (Table 4 ). The columns mentioned in Table 4, signify the allocating positions of the interacting parameters. The impact of the interaction between two factors is represented with SI index, which is a numerical value. Qualitek-4software package computed the value of SI index by measuring the angle between the positions of two factors allocated in the column. When two factors will be perpendicular to each other in their position in column, the value of SI index will be considered as 100%. The value of SI index will be zero percent when the lines will be parallel. Therefore, the SI index varies from 0 to 100 as the angle between two factors diverges between 0 and 90˚. Reserved columns represent that it must be preserved during the evaluation of interaction characteristics. The levels designated for each parameter are indicated by levels which are required for the optimal parametric conditions [30]. It is found from Table 4 the strongest interaction (SI 51.37%) is detected between solid to liquid ratio and pH for PC extraction, followed by electrical acoustic intensity and pH (SI 44.18%), solid to liquid ratio and duty cycle (SI 36.61%), duty cycle and electrical acoustic intensity (SI 23.44%), solid to liquid ratio and electrical acoustic intensity (SI 19.77%), Duty cycle and pH (SI 1.71%) (Table 4). Table 3 indicates that pH has the strongest influence, followed by electrical acoustic intensity (high influence parameter), solid to liquid ratio (modest influence parameter) and duty cycle (lowest influence parameter) for enhanced PC extraction. It is evident that solid to liquid ratio is a modest influence parameter (Table 3), but displays a higher SI with a combination of pH. Electrical acoustic intensity and pH are high and highest influence parameters respectively in Table 3; however, they illustrate the second maximum severity index in a combination of them. Furthermore, solid to liquid ratio and duty cycle are modest, and the lowest influence parameter (Table 2), third-highest severity index was found in their combination during PC extraction.

For, PE extraction, in combination of both solid to liquid ratio and pH display highest interaction (SI 51.02%), followed by solid to liquid ratio and electrical acoustic intensity (SI 38%), solid to liquid ratio and duty cycle (SI 37.45%), electrical acoustic intensity and pH (SI 35.38%), duty cycle and electrical acoustic intensity (SI 21.58%), Duty cycle X pH (SI 4.31%) (Table 4). Table 3 indicates that pH is the highest influence parameter followed by electrical acoustic intensity (high influence parameter), duty cycle (modest influence parameter) and solid to liquid ratio (lowest influence parameter) for enhanced PE extraction. It is found from Table 3 that solid to liquid ratio is the lowest influence parameter but illustrates a highest (SI 51.02%), second highest (SI 38%) and third-highest SI (SI 37.45%) in combination with pH, electrical acoustic intensity and duty cycle respectively. Table 3 shows that pH and electrical acoustic intensity are high and highest influence parameters respectively; however, they depict comparatively lower SI (SI 35.38%) in combination.

It is apparent from Table 4, the strongest interaction (SI 46.26%) is found between solid to liquid ratio and pH for APC extraction, followed by electrical acoustic intensity and pH (SI 44.76%), duty cycle and electrical acoustic intensity (SI 31.37%), solid to liquid ratio and electrical acoustic intensity (SI 25.40%), solid to liquid ratio and duty cycle (SI 25.37%), Duty cycle X pH (SI 1.54%) (Table 4). Table 3 indicates that pH is the highest influence parameter, followed by electrical acoustic intensity (high influence parameter), duty cycle (modest influence parameter) and solid to liquid ratio (lowest influence parameter) for enhanced APC extraction. It is evident that solid to liquid ratio is the lowest influence parameter (Table 3), however, displays the highest SI with pH in combination. Electrical acoustic intensity and pH are high and highest influence parameters respectively in Table 3, but, they illustrate the second-highest severity index in combination. Furthermore, solid to liquid ratio and duty cycle are modest and lowest influence parameters, respectively (Table 2), third moderate severity index was found in their combination during APC extraction. Patil & Akamanchi, (2017) investigated the influence of various process parameters on the extraction of camptothecin (CPT) from Nothapodytes nimmoniana [12]. The extraction was carried out using UAE. They reported that electric-acoustic intensity, solid to liquid ratio and duty cycle played a crucial role in the extraction yield of CPT [12]. Kumar and Banerjee, (2019) also performed lipid extraction from Trichosporon sp. using UAE [31]. It is evident that both frequency and power showed a significant contribution to lipid yield. It is also apparent from Table 4 that the yield of PBPs is quite self-governing of distinct impacts but reliant on the interaction of parameters.

The experimental data obtained from various trials were examined using analysis of variance (ANOVA) to find out the contribution of an individual parameter on the extraction of PBPs. ANOVA results along with the percentage of contribution of each parameter are given in Table 5 . Here, the contribution of each factor including interaction effect is the percentage ratio of sums of squares of individual factor to total sums of squares. Duty cycle contributed the maximum influence (30.81%) on PC extraction followed by solid–liquid ratio (28.62%), pH (22.46%) and electrical acoustic intensity (18.10%). Additionally, the highest contribution on the extraction of PE was found for pH, followed by duty cycle, solid to liquid ratio, and electrical acoustic intensity. Furthermore, pH, duty cycle, and solid to liquid ratio contribute almost equal impact on APC extraction followed by electrical acoustic intensity (Table 5). The p-value indicates the gravity of fringe implication and possibility of the incidence of an assigned event to have occurred. Theabove statistical value can also be used for the elimination of factors due to the low level of importance. In the present study, the p-value is found within the significance limit (less than 0.05); therefore, the contribution of all factors is found significant on the extraction of PC, PE, and APC. Chen et al. [32] determined the optimal level of process factor to extracting of the oil from ginger by Taguchi methodology. It was evident that the calculated p-value of each factor concerning their contribution was less than 0.05 [32]. In the present study, each factor individually influenced the extraction of PBPs at a certain level. However, this important parameter contributes to a greater yield of PBPs when these parameters act jointly, as these parameters show pronounced interactive effects.

The optimal level of each important parameter with their respective contribution to achieving maximum yield of PBPs is reported in Table 6 . The optimal condition is determined using two-steps methods for optimization. Initially, the level of each factor is adjusted to decrease the value of variability. Further, parameters are accustomed to increase the value of the mean. The reduction of variability will always be highlighted if higher mean value contributes greater variability for any factor. It is apparent from Table 6 that pH exhibits the highest impact on PC extraction followed by electrical acoustic intensity, solid–liquid ratio, and duty cycle. pH also shows a significant role for both PE and APC extraction, followed by duty cycle, solid–liquid ratio, and electrical acoustic intensity. The highest yield of PBPs predicted by Taguchi methodology can be achieved with solid–liquid ratio (0.2 g/ml), duty cycle (75%), electrical acoustic intensity (16.99 w/cm2) and pH 7. Patil & Akamanchi, (2017) evaluated the optimal level of various process factors required for UAE to attain maximum yield of CPT. It was reported that the higher yield of CPT was found with a solid to liquid ratio of 0.017 g/ml−1, and electric-acoustic intensity of 191 Wcm−2 [12]. Kumar and Banerjee, (2019) reported that 50 Hz of frequency and 2800 W of power is required to achieve a higher yield of lipid from Trichosporon sp. using UAE [31]. Since the porosity of solid is varied with biomass used for extraction, therefore, various level of process parameters are required to achieve a higher yield of products. The optimum conditions for anticipated extraction of PC, PE, and APC, are found where S/N ratio is 42.45, 29.72 and 40.85 respectively. The estimated yield of PC, PE and APC from the S/N ratio is 132.59, 30.62 and 110.28 mg/g respectively under UAE [by Eq. (3)].

Fig. 2 illustrates the frequency distribution graphs plotted for current and improved conditions of PC, PE, and APC. It has been found that yield of PC may be increased from 68.31 mg/g (S/N ratio is 36.69) to 132.59 mg/g (S/N ratio is 42.45) after UAE. Overall 94.1% enhancement of yield of PC may be increased (Fig. 2a). Additionally, Taguchi methodology also predicts that the yield of PE may be enhanced from 15.69 mg/g to 30.62 mg/g as S/N ratio may be increased from 23.91 to 29.72. Therefore, overall, 95.20% of PE yield may be increased after the UAE under optimized condition (Fig. 2b). Furthermore, enhancement of APC yield may be possible from 57.88 mg/g to 110.28 mg/g under optimized condition during UAE. Overall, 90.54% enhancement of yield of APC may be increased (Fig. 2c). Further, to authenticate the planned methodology, the experiments engaging the obtained optimized extraction conditions were carried out. The results indicate that optimized extraction conditions amplified the yield of PC, PE, and APC up to 131.45 mg/g (92.42%), 30.10 mg/g (91.89%) and 108.24 mg/g (87.01%) respectively. The overall aberration of expected results from experiment datawas found within the range of validation [33]. In our previous experiment, 0.05 M phosphate buffer was used for extraction of PC, PE, and APC from homogenized Oscillatoria sp. BTA 170 [20] and yield was reported as 94.58 mg/g, 24.91 mg/g and 96.70 mg/g for PC, PE and APC respectively. In the present study, 38.99%, 20.84%, and 11.93% of higher yield of PC, PE, and APC were attained after UAE under optimized condition.

A batch UAE of PC, PE, and APC was carried out with 0.2 g/ml of solid–liquid ratio, 75% of duty cycle, 16.99 w/cm2 of electrical acoustic intensity and pH 7. The batch extraction of PBPs was performed for 20 min at room temperature using 1 g of lyophilized powder of cyanobacterial biomass. The sample was collected after the 2-minute interval and amount of PC, PE, and APC were measured. The data obtained from various batch experiments were fitted using Graph Pad Prism 5 software package with Eq. (4). It has been found that the dissociation rate of PC, PE, and APC from cyanobacterial biomass was fitted well with the second-order model. After the integration of Eq. (4), a linear relationship between t/ct vs. t (Eq. (6)), was used to calculate the value of Cs and k.
(6)
t
C t
=
1
k
C S 2
+
t
C t

The relationship of t/ct vs. t from batch experimental data of PC, PE, and APC is illustrated in Fig. 3 . It is evident that higher regression values for PC, PE, and APC was obtained. Therefore, the second-order extraction model may be applied to explain the extraction process under UAE. The reciprocal of the slope of a straight line between t/ct vs t is saturated concentration (Cs). The values of Cs for PC, PE, and APC were computed as 59.88, 10.35, and 97.09 g/l. Results show that APC has higher solubility, followed by PC and PE. Since the solubility of protein at particular pH is reliant on the ionized groups available in the surface of them. The variation of solubility at pH-7 indicates that the isoelectric point of APC is close to neutral pH or more hydrophilic amino acids are available on the surface of APC. The second-order rate constant value was calculated from the intercept of a straight line between t/ct vs. t and values of k for PC, PE and APC were computed as 6.66 × 10−4, 64.09 × 10−4 and 1.49 × 10−4 L/mg/min respectively. It is evident that the rate constant value for PE was found to be higher when compared with PC and APC, however, rate of extraction was found higher for APC than other PBPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of antioxidant property and scavenging activity of PBPs
ce:section.ce:para: The antioxidant potential of PBPs has carried out through dose-dependent activity where the standard was used ascorbic acid. Fig. 4 a illustrates the antioxidant properties of PBPs. Results depict that antioxidant property of PBPs is increased as the dose is increased. Results indicate that antioxidant potentiality of 100, 200, 500, 700 and 1000 µg containing of PBPs was found to be 0.146, 0.297, 0.488, 0.587 and 1.12 where the potentiality of the antioxidant property was expressed concerning absorbance at 695 nm. Since the antioxidant property of PBPs depends on its composition and amount of PBPs present in solution, therefore, a variation of absorbance was found during experiments. Furthermore, the antioxidant potential for 10 µg of ascorbic acid, 500 µg and 1000 µg of PBPs were found to be 0.53, 0.587 and 1.12 respectively when compared with regard to the obtained value of absorbance at 695 nm. The above finding shows that PBPs had moderate antioxidant capacity when comparison of data was performed with respect to ascorbic acid. The capability of different concentrations of PBSs was evaluated to scavenge hydrogen peroxide, and results were compared with gallic acid. Since hydrogen peroxide is an oxidizing agent, therefore, reducing characteristics of PBPs was evaluated in the existence of hydrogen peroxide in the present experiment. The percentage of scavenging activity was computed using Eq. (4) and results are given in Fig. 4b. Results displayed that scavenging activity of galic acid, was found to be 29.55, 43.26, and 71.77 in the presence of 10, 30 and 50 µg of galic acid respectively. It is evident that the said activity increased in a dose-dependent manner. However, 100, 300 and 500 µg containing PBPs showed 25.11, 72.90, and 129.84 scavenging activity when reading was computed using Eq. (5). The variation of scavenging properties of PBPs and galic acid is due to their composition. Comparative higher scavenging properties was noticed (Fig. 4b) for galic acid, which may be due to the availability of more functional group in compare with PBPs. Hydroxyl radicals are the key parameter in leaving organisms for triggering the aging and damage of tissue and could fuel the evolution of many diseases associated with degeneration. Additionally, the prevalence of superoxide anion can strengthen the injury of the cell as it generates other kinds of radicals, which act as mediators for oxidizing agents [34,35]. It is evident from the results that the PBPs of Oscillatoria sp. BTA170 portentously affects the scavenging of hydroxyl free radical.

In the present study, a statistical optimization tool has been used to optimize the various important process parameters for quick development of ultrasound assisted batch extraction process to achieve the higher yield of PBPs from Oscillatoria sp. (BTA 170). A layout for nine batch extraction trials has been designed by Taguchi methodology. The option ‘bigger is better’ of signal to noise ratio (S/N) has been used to analyze the batch extraction data for evaluation of the individual impact of each process factor and their corresponding interactive influences. The optimum level of each factor and a corresponding yield of PC, PE, and APC have been also predicted by planned methodology. The batch extraction data acquired at optimized experimental condition has been compared with the yield of PBPs predicted by methodology. The close resemblance of data obtained from the experiment and Taguchi methodology indicates that this methodology can be used to explain the batch extraction process. The time-dependent results acquired from ultrasound assisted batch extraction of PBPs have been fitted well with the second-order kinetic model. PBPs displayed noteworthy scavenging and antioxidant activity; therefore, Oscillatoria sp. (BTA 170) can be considered as a potential feedstock for various industrial applications.
This article does not contain any studies with human participants or animals performed by any of the authors.
Authors would like to acknowledge to DBT, Govt. of India for financial support (BT/PR25035/ NER/95/ 973/ 2017).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This material is based upon research work supported by ICAR-IARI, New Delhi and NIT Agartala. Dr. O N Tiwari articulates thankfulness to HoD, Division of Microbiology of ICAR-IARI, New Delhi for inspiration and support. Dr. Oinam and Dr. Indrama would like to acknowledge to Director, DBT-IBSD, Imphal. Dr. Tarun kanti Bandyopadhyay, Dr. Abhijit Mondal and Dr. Biswanath Bhunia express gratitude to Director, National Institute of Technology Agartala for encouragement and support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ultsonch.2019.104762.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
